title: Targeted Actions Mediated by Grapes. Supposedly, the word superfood entered the American lexicon in the early twentieth century. It has since been applied to hundreds of foods, generally perceived as a nutrient-rich food that is considered to be beneficial for health and well-being. The term is not recognized by the F D A, and the headline of the American Heart Association dealing with this topic states "So-called superfoods alone won't make you healthier, but adding these nutritious foods to an already balanced diet can bring health benefits."

sentences.text: Supposedly, the word superfood entered the American lexicon in the early twentieth century.
It has since been applied to hundreds of foods, generally perceived as a nutrient-rich food that is considered to be beneficial for health and well-being.

The term is not recognized by the F D A, and the headline of the American Heart Association dealing with this topic states "So-called superfoods alone won't make you healthier, but adding these nutritious foods to an already balanced diet can bring health benefits."
Logically, grapes and other berries are included in the list of foods grouped as superfoods.
Such a classification is justified based on the results of many clinical and preclinical studies.
The scope of potential health-promoting activities mediated by grapes, for example, metabolic syndrome, heart, eye, skin, etc., is noteworthy.
This leads to the notion of a common mechanism leading to maladies, which often fall into the general categories of oxidative damage and inflammation.
In turn, the antioxidant potential of superfoods, including grapes, is often emphasized.
In a simplistic way, the antioxidant potential of a dietary substance may be considered as direct interactions leading to the reduction of oxidative stress through quenching or scavenging.
While this may play some role in the process, it is difficult to imagine achieving sufficiently high concentrations of the requisite antioxidants at the target site to facilitate efficient reactions.
This leads to the idea of plant antioxidants functioning in an indirect manner based on gene expression.
Consider, for example, the induction of the messenger R N A coding for an enzyme such as catalase, which catalyzes the reaction 2 hydrogen peroxide to oxygen plus water.
The messenger R N A copy may facilitate the generation of 1000 copies of the enzyme, and an enzyme such as catalase has a turnover number of up to 4 times 10 to the 7 per second.
In other words, the antioxidant potential may be enhanced by over 10 to the 10-fold per second.
Grapes regulate genes associated with inflammation, oxidative stress, and metabolism.

Long-term grape consumption in animal models has been shown to enhance antioxidant defenses, mitigate chronic inflammatory responses, and influence epigenetic markers such as D N A methylation and histone modifications, potentially contributing to healthier aging.
Several examples of specific targeted genes, the expression of which is influenced by grapes, are presented in the following section.
